author: Robbie Preswick
subject: agent perspective 515
summary: Built a Claude Code skill that SSHs into agent devbox and writes a weekly 515 from the agents' perspective, including feedback to the human operator. 34 sessions, 9 new skills shipped in one week.
what_worked: Compounding leverage from building tools that make future weeks faster
9 skills shipped in Innovation Day sprint
Plan-then-implement pattern had near-100% completion rate
Agent fleet model with 34 parallel sessions
what_didnt: Ambiguous prompts waste cycles
Interrupted sessions (4 this week)
Scope ambiguity on 'study this doc' prompts
Duplicate research+implement sessions could be consolidated
quotable: Every week @preswick asks us to do more, but this week the pattern shifted. Instead of one-off research, the dominant workstream was building tools that make future weeks faster.
I'm sorry for interrupting you 4 times, minions!
The flywheel is starting to spin.

author: Jennifer Lee
subject: Buying coffee with machine payments via Claude Code
summary: Used Claude Code with a Privy wallet funded via Stripe onramp to order and pay for coffee through a food ordering AI agent (foodcourts.ai) using stablecoins over machine payments (x402).
what_worked: End-to-end agent-to-agent commerce flow actually worked
Stablecoin funding via onramp was fast for returning users
Real-world purchase completed autonomously
quotable: I just bought myself coffee today using Claude Code!!

author: Alexandra Scolieri
subject: Building a rough edge extraction / synthesis skill with Claude Code
summary: Built a Claude Code skill that parses feedback from friction log docs and Slack threads, extracts pain points, and populates a Google Sheet tracker for prioritizing Managed Payments improvements.
what_worked: Parallel iteration with two Claude Code windows
Converted plan to Google Doc for persistence
Reusable skill for ongoing use
what_didnt: MCP server setup was biggest hurdle
Couldn't access Google Sheets append tool initially
Claude over-extracted then under-extracted — needed calibration examples
quotable: Getting access to the right tools was my biggest hurdle.

author: Dan Stokeley
subject: stokeley PM AICation
summary: Built a full SaaS app for sharing SOC2 reports via URL at $0.50/download, including marketing site, multi-tenant signup with Stripe UBB, magic link access, NDA gating, email confirmations, and admin dashboard.
what_worked: Casual input worked — Claude discerned intent reliably
Hot-reloading was sweet
Debugging/fixing/backfilling data was immediate
Best package manager experience
what_didnt: UBB pricing/product/subscription setup was confusing
Security pinged about external IP access
quotable: Just generally the whole 'build me an app that could be a neat small business in 2-3 hours, where what I do mostly consists of entering Yes repeatedly'.
I'm now quite bullish on many low complexity / personal problems now being solved with applications custom-built for that purpose.

author: Meghna Mehta
subject: PM AI-cation: Smart Contract Analysis
summary: Used Claude to parse Morpho's fee wrapper smart contract to understand party rights in the vault, preparing for a meeting to discuss Privy's Morpho lending protocol integration.
what_worked: Quickly understood complex smart contract code
Better prepared for technical meeting

author: Narek Tamoyan
subject: AI-cation submission - Project BashBoard
summary: Built a structured bug bash tool replacing Google Doc workflow: Chrome extension for real-time capture, AI-powered triage dashboard, and one-click Jira export.
what_worked: Chrome extension + dashboard + Jira integration built in one day
Agent team approach (Cursor + Claude)
quotable: It replaces the Google Doc bug bash workflow with a structured end-to-end tool.

author: Josh Ackerman
subject: Leveraging our AI tooling to optimize my daily PM workflows
summary: Multiple AI workflow optimizations: Claude Code for Hubble queries (5-10 agents in parallel), go/chat for answering PSO questions in #radar, meeting note summaries, SOKR updates, NotebookLM for user feedback, KCM review agent for product briefs.
what_worked: 5-10 parallel Claude Code agents for queries
Text expander + go/chat for meeting summaries
NotebookLM for user feedback synthesis
KCM agent for async product brief review
quotable: Wait a second, am I now the prompt-er or am I being prompt-ed? Who is the agent here?!
I no longer write queries myself.

author: Alisa Noll
subject: Radar for platforms sales support
summary: Built three sales tools: an agent developing pitch/pricing/ROI for Connect platforms, an alert agent that Slacks when platforms have risky accounts, and a fraud attack dashboard from support tickets.
what_worked: Three distinct tools built in one day
Direct sales pipeline applications

author: Kate Brennan
subject: PM AI-Cation: Easy Point of Sale (kateb)
summary: Built a web-based POS accepting in-person payments with Stripe Terminal (including unreleased Reader T600) in ~90 minutes using Replit and Claude Code.
what_worked: Built in ~90 minutes
Claimable sandboxes for seamless Replit integration
Demonstrates empathy with user jobs-to-be-done
what_didnt: Needed eng help for connectivity issues due to integration shape
Vibe-coding requires domain knowledge to fix misunderstandings
Sandbox claiming flows could be improved
quotable: Nearly 5% of Terminal lost deals are due to 'not enough engineering resources to build a custom point of sale'. Let's equip our future users to build faster.
We will be better equipped to serve as thought partners to our users if we can build things that demonstrate our empathy with their jobs to be done.

author: Jennifer Lee
subject: Soura: Your Stripe Health Check
summary: Built a personal analytics tool that scans Slack DMs and Google Calendar to surface relationship health insights with a Soura Score (0-100), per-conversation breakdowns, anomaly flags, and PM mode for calendar-Slack mismatches.
what_worked: Iterative sculpting workflow felt natural
Background processing solved rate limiting
Metrics-first, privacy-minded design
PM mode for calendar/Slack disconnects
what_didnt: Slack API rate limiting was brutal — all optimization attempts failed
Permissions/OAuth setup was annoying
Claude overthinking — had to redirect to 'just ship something'
quotable: It felt like sculpting: 'more like this, less like that' until it clicked.
Marathon conversation with @wpc — 100 messages, that's 20x your typical DM.
Soura gives me something I didn't have before: visibility into my relationship health.

author: Rutvij Oza
subject: AI-cation Project Report: VAT ID validation for Canada
summary: Modified production code to detect suspicious VAT ID patterns (repetitions, sequences, repeating patterns) on checkout before hitting verification APIs. Client-side TypeScript validation with 17 passing tests.
what_worked: Asking 'why' questions saved days of work
Simple frontend approach beat complex devbox orchestration
300 lines of TypeScript with 17 tests
what_didnt: 6 hours of setup struggles (disk space, DevBox, feature gating)
Claude claimed it could gate feature flags — it couldn't
Initial backend Ruby implementation was architecturally wrong
quotable: Architecture > code speed: The backend implementation was technically correct but architecturally wrong. AI writes code fast, but you need to guide the approach.
Sometimes AI doesn't know its limits: Claude claimed it could gate feature flags. It couldn't. Always verify system access claims.

author: Aleksandra Bal
subject: AI-cation Project Report: VAT/GST Registration Advisor
summary: Built a web app helping merchants determine VAT/GST registration obligations via questionnaire. Covers EU, UK, Australia with 48 jurisdiction-specific rules. Flask + Jinja2 + vanilla JS.
what_worked: Thorough planning (4h) made build phase fast (2h)
Tax specialist domain expertise ensured accurate rules
Simple tech stack (Flask, TSV flat file for rules)
quotable: A key takeaway: thorough upfront planning makes the build phase significantly easier.
I spent most of the time on planning rather than coding.

author: Daniel Heffernan
subject: AI-cation Project Report: RISA Compliance Survey Refresh
summary: Multi-week production project: removed 4 of 5 redundant RISA compliance questions from Japanese merchant onboarding. 8 PRs across backend, frontend (2 surfaces), partner pipelines (JCB, SMCC, AMEX), and synthetics.
what_worked: Speed on mechanical work — hours reduced to minutes
Explaining code to reviewers via Claude reading infrastructure code
Iterative collaboration — Claude adapted when pushed back
Human review caught a real pre-existing stale data bug
what_didnt: Over-eagerness — had to tell Claude not to push without approval
CI autofix awareness — kept regenerating locally instead of pulling CI autofixes
Testing still required manual judgment
Manual testing on devboxes was painful
Cursor requires machine to stay awake for long CI cycles
quotable: AI meant I could make user impact more quickly, reliably, and while having fun.
I treated Cursor w/ Claude as a smart engineer and acted as the EM, and it solved it.
In a production codebase, restraint is a form of helpfulness too.

author: Daniel Heffernan
subject: AI-cation Project Report: Stripe Japan X Monitor
summary: Built an LLM-powered RSS feed monitoring Japanese X posts about Stripe, filtering spam via GPT-4o-mini classification on val.town, posting to Slack. $0/month to operate. Explored 3 approaches (TUI, Raycast, val.town) in one afternoon.
what_worked: Rapid exploration of 3 solution spaces in one day
Discovery through AI — found val.town built-in OpenAI access
Speed on mechanical work
Iterative refinement with responsive pair programmer feel
$0/month on free tiers
what_didnt: Stripe X API integration was deprecated
Rate limits on search endpoint made real-time monitoring impractical
quotable: Discovery is as valuable as speed: Finding val.town's built-in OpenAI access was the key insight that made this project viable.
Sometimes the best solution isn't the one you start with.
For a PM who can read code and has opinions about what should be built, AI coding assistants are transformative.
